answer:
**Charles Dunoyer's conception of *liberté* (liberty or freedom) is fundamentally unitary: it denotes *power* or *facility of action* acquired by progressively removing obstacles—internal (e.g., ignorance, vice) or external (e.g., violence, unjust restrictions)—that impede the exercise of faculties.[^1]** This definition, elaborated at length in the opening chapter of *Nouveau traité d'économie*, Vol. I, applies equally to individuals and collectives (e.g., peoples, societies, or branches of activity like "industry"), without substantive distinction in its metaphysical handling.[^2] 

Dunoyer explicitly rejects rival views (e.g., Rousseau's "natural" liberty of the savage, Bentham's equation of liberty with license) that might bifurcate individual vs. collective senses, insisting instead that liberty scales with *civilization*: development of faculties, moral habits (personal and social), and removal of impediments.[^3] For individuals, it is "the power we have acquired to use our forces," measured by industry, instruction, and justice toward self/others.[^4] Collectively—for a society or "industry" (*l'industrie*)—it denotes the analogous *power* of that aggregate activity: the capacity of producers (or "industrious" persons) to satisfy needs freely via division of labor, capital accumulation, and unobstructed exchange.[^5]

### Collective: *La liberté de l'industrie* (Liberty/Freedom of an Industry)
Dunoyer routinely employs *liberté* as a collective noun for "industry" (*l'industrie*), denoting its systemic power/facility to produce amid competition, free from monopolies, regulations, or violence. In Vol. II, Ch. 14 ("On the conditions under which all industry can be free"), he analyzes *liberté* as industry's progressive emancipation: accumulation of "personal" (faculties, virtues) and "real" (tools, capital) means, enabling vast output.[^6] E.g., good habits (*morals*) liberate industry by curbing vice (laziness, prodigality) and fostering justice (no monopolies).[^7] 

In Ch. 16 ("On the liberty of the manufacturing industry"), *liberté de l'industrie manufacturière* means manufacturing's power to transform matter (via machines, division of labor) without guild prohibitions or tariffs—obstacles that historically enslaved it.[^8] Here, collective liberty emerges from individual virtues aggregated at scale (e.g., emulation in factories), yielding "ever greater" confidence/power as obstacles fall.[^9] It is not "license" but *civilized power*: e.g., free competition crushes monopolies, extending markets.[^10]

### Individual Capacity
The *same* definition governs persons: liberty is personal power grown via self-mastery (e.g., sobriety, skill) and justice (no harm to others).[^11] Industry's collective liberty is thus *composed* of individuals' capacities: "a people can enjoy an immense liberty before... self-government" if its members develop "much knowledge, industry, capital, good... habits."[^12] No collective liberty without individual (e.g., workers' vices paralyze factories; virtues empower them).[^13]

Dunoyer thus handles *la liberté* *consistently*: collective "freedom of an industry" is the emergent power of industrious individuals cooperating justly, scaled to societal activity. Divergence arises only in application (personal vices vs. monopolies), not essence—both require obstacle-removal for maximal action.[^14]

[^1]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie*, Vol. I, Ch. 1 ("What the author means by the word liberty"), [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-fc8999).
[^2]: *Ibid.*, [§1](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-3922ae) (debates free will irrelevant; liberty = acquired power).
[^3]: *Ibid.*, [§5](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-709973) (critiques Rousseau, Constituent Assembly, Bentham).
[^4]: *Ibid.*, [§3](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-ca2a56) (development, self-restraint, non-harm to others).
[^5]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie*, Vol. II, Ch. 14 ("On the conditions under which all industry can be free"), [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/14-iv-des-conditions-auxquelles-toute-industrie-peut-/translation/).
[^6]: *Ibid.*, [§4](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/14-iv-des-conditions-auxquelles-toute-industrie-peut-/translation/#p-d372b7) (industry's "power and liberty" from personal/real capital).
[^7]: *Ibid.*, [§7-8](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/14-iv-des-conditions-auxquelles-toute-industrie-peut-/translation/#p-85d580).
[^8]: Vol. II, Ch. 16 ("On the liberty of the manufacturing industry"), [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/16-vi-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-manufacturiere/translation/#p-4b6a43).
[^9]: *Ibid.*, [§8](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/16-vi-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-manufacturiere/translation/#p-a10324).
[^10]: *Ibid.*, [§6](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/16-vi-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-manufacturiere/translation/#p-32870d) (guilds, prohibitions as obstacles).
[^11]: Vol. I, Ch. 1, [§4](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-b8546d).
[^12]: *Ibid.*, [§6](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-132c59).
[^13]: Vol. II, Ch. 14, [§8](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/14-iv-des-conditions-auxquelles-toute-industrie-peut-/translation/#p-8b7b78) (vices enervate industry).
[^14]: The corpus yields no explicit contrast; uniformity is key (e.g., no "collective liberty" as mere sum, but emergent from individual power scaled via civilization).
